text: One by one, U.S. civil rights agency dismantles tools to fight discrimination
label: SUBJ

text: In 1966, the newly-established Equal Employment Opportunity Commission issued a rule to tackle entrenched discrimination on the job.
label: OBJ

text: Every year, companies with a hundred or more workers would turn over to the government information about the race, ethnicity, sex and job categories of their employees.
label: OBJ

text: This EEO-1 data, as it's known, has helped the federal agency figure out where people of color and women are not getting hired or promoted.
label: OBJ

text: Over decades, the EEOC's work has led to settlements worth billions.
label: OBJ

text: Now, as part of a realignment of civil rights enforcement under President Trump, the EEOC is seeking to end its annual data collection while also getting rid of a 1979 regulation that allowed employers to take certain steps to address race and gender imbalances revealed by the data.
label: OBJ

text: Together, the moves would mark an about-face in the civil rights agency's efforts to fulfill its mission.
label: SUBJ

text: Andrea Lucas, the Trump-appointed chair of the EEOC, did not respond to [OUTLET]'s questions about the two proposals, which have been submitted to the White House for review.
label: OBJ

text: But in interviews and public remarks, Lucas has repeatedly warned that programs or policies aimed at helping specific groups, such as Black people or women, are unlawful under Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 if they exclude others.
label: OBJ

text: "Regardless of what has happened before, the way to stop discriminating based on race is to stop discriminating based on race. The end.
label: SUBJ

text: The end. Full stop," Lucas said at the Fortune Workplace Innovation Summit earlier this month.
label: OBJ

text: "I think that that's a more beautiful vision of our country, and I think it's consistent with the text of the statute."
label: OBJ

text: A roadmap for addressing discrimination
label: OBJ

text: The 1979 regulation the EEOC seeks to rescind was issued with this very dilemma in mind: Can a company remedy discrimination by giving special consideration to those who were deprived of opportunities in the past?
label: SUBJ

text: The answer back then was yes.
label: OBJ

text: The agency gave the go-ahead for mentoring programs and even hiring targets.
label: OBJ

text: "The EEOC says you can take some of these voluntary efforts, even though they will be race- or gender-conscious," says Chai Feldblum, who served on the commission during the Obama and first Trump administrations.
label: OBJ

text: "This is the EEOC giving employers the roadmap of how they can take race and gender into account in a positive way and not violate the law."
label: OBJ

text: The guidelines, issued in January 1979, made clear that companies first had to document a problem, and then come up with a reasonable and time-limited plan for how to increase the number of minorities or women in their ranks.
label: OBJ

text: Five months later, the Supreme Court embraced that roadmap.
label: OBJ

text: In a 5-2 decision known as Weber, the court found that an affirmative action plan to remedy past discrimination was lawful provided it did not "unnecessarily trammel the interests of white employees" and that it was temporary.
label: OBJ

text: In 1987, the court issued another decision, known as Johnson, extending protection to efforts aimed at helping women.
label: OBJ

text: Now known as the Weber-Johnson standard, it's still the law regardless of what happens with the EEOC's 1979 regulation, says Feldblum.
label: OBJ

text: But for how long, she's not sure.
label: OBJ

text: "I think the Supreme Court is just waiting for a case that might allow them to overturn those two important cases," she says.
label: OBJ

text: How data has helped root out discrimination
label: OBJ

text: The more imminent change, assuming the EEOC's proposals go forward, is the demise of the agency's annual collection of employee demographics.
label: SUBJ

text: Usually, the data collection begins in late spring.
label: OBJ

text: So far this year, there's been no word of it.
label: OBJ

text: Since the 1960s, the EEOC has recovered billions of dollars for workers who have suffered discrimination on the job, and in many cases, EEO-1 data played a key role.
label: OBJ

text: "It's one of the first things that you can look at as you're trying to learn more," says Karla Gilbride, who served as the EEOC's general counsel during the Biden administration.
label: OBJ

text: Protecting U.S. workers from unlawful discrimination — already a hard task — could become significantly harder if the government no longer has that data within arm's reach, Gilbride says.
label: OBJ

text: Having to subpoena data would make enforcement far more laborious and less efficient.
label: SUBJ

text: A lawsuit against Bass Pro Shops
label: OBJ

text: Consider the lawsuit against Bass Pro Shops, first filed in 2011.
label: OBJ

text: The EEOC alleged the company, formally known as Bass Pro Outdoor World, discriminated against Black and Hispanic job applicants by not hiring them — not just at one store, but across the country, even in places with sizable Black and Hispanic populations.
label: OBJ

text: "Store by store by store, sort of the same idea, where you had areas that had a significant number of Blacks and Latinos, and either zero or very few at the stores," says David Lopez, who was the EEOC's general counsel at the time and now leads the Civil Rights, Migration and Workplace Law Initiative at Arizona State University.
label: OBJ

text: The EEOC saw that pattern because it had Bass Pro's demographic data on file.
label: OBJ

text: Government investigators could easily compare the outdoor gear shop to other retailers in the same counties.
label: OBJ

text: They could also compare Bass Pro's workforce to the available pool of workers in the surrounding areas.
label: OBJ

text: While the data by itself could not prove discrimination, Lopez says it was a green light to agency investigators to dig further.
label: OBJ

text: "Because they had a reason to investigate, they were able to discover that there were managerial comments that were reflective of discriminatory animus, that they were looking for a certain type of person," says Lopez.
label: OBJ

text: Someone who was white, according to the government's complaint.
label: OBJ

text: Bass Pro called the allegations "threadbare" and accused the government of merely relying on "a handful of isolated incidents of alleged inappropriate behavior."
label: OBJ

text: EEOC investigators later bolstered their case, identifying implicated managers and job applicants by name and compiling a list of dozens of Bass Pro stores with a low representation of Black and Hispanic employees.
label: OBJ

text: Finally, in 2017, the company settled for $10.5 million.
label: OBJ

text: Bass Pro did not admit to any wrongdoing, but agreed to appoint a diversity director and to make good-faith efforts to recruit and hire non-white candidates.
label: OBJ

text: Lopez considered the settlement a big win, one of many he oversaw in his time at the EEOC that were built on data.
label: OBJ

text: "You can have a hunch, but there's nothing like the cold, hard numbers," he says.
label: OBJ

text: Agency chair says data has been misused
label: OBJ

text: Early indications of the EEOC's plan to stop gathering data came a year ago.
label: OBJ

text: In announcing the opening of the 2025 data collection period, Lucas posted a message warning employers of their obligations under federal civil rights law.
label: OBJ

text: "You must not use the information collected and reported in your organization's EEO-1 Component 1 report to justify treating employees differently based on their race, sex, or other protected characteristic," she wrote.
label: OBJ

text: In an interview with [OUTLET] earlier this year, Lucas explained her missive.
label: OBJ

text: She said a number of companies have been misusing the data — including in ways that have hurt white people and men.
label: OBJ

text: Lucas believes the only people who should know the gender and race of a company's employees are its lawyers and human resources staff.
label: OBJ

text: Instead, after the 2020 murder of George Floyd by a white police officer, a number of companies published their demographic data as part of public commitments to address the lack of diversity within their ranks.
label: OBJ

text: Subsequently, she contends, companies began making decisions about whom to hire, promote and interview for jobs based on sex or race, noting some even gave hiring managers financial incentives to hit diversity targets.
label: OBJ

text: That use of demographic data crosses the line, she says.
label: OBJ

text: "All it has to do is motivate — in whole or in part — your decision making, and you're into unlawful territory."
label: OBJ

text: Lucas declined to single out any company by name, citing the confidentiality of agency investigations.
label: OBJ

text: But according to court documents, the EEOC has accused Nike and The New York Times of discrimination against white employees and job applicants.
label: OBJ

text: The two companies are among many that published their demographic data along with their diversity-related goals for several years.
label: OBJ

text: A focus on data in select cases
label: OBJ

text: Paradoxically, Lucas has at times talked up the importance of data.
label: OBJ

text: "There is no other way to protect victims of harassment and discrimination unless you collect information about them," she said while speaking in April at a conference at Harvard organized by the Brandeis Center, an independent civil rights organization.
label: OBJ

text: In that instance, she was defending the EEOC's subpoena, requiring the University of Pennsylvania to turn over employee information that the agency doesn't routinely collect: the names, addresses and phone numbers of Jewish employees who may have witnessed antisemitic acts on campus.
label: OBJ

text: The university has, so far, refused to comply with the subpoena, noting in court filings that it echoes terrifying periods of history for Jewish communities.
label: OBJ

text: "Driving a car without a dashboard"
label: OBJ

text: The profound changes underway at the EEOC have kept David Cohen busy.
label: SUBJ

text: The president of the management consulting firm DCI Consulting has fielded many calls from confused clients, wondering whether the work they've been doing to promote equal opportunity should continue.
label: OBJ

text: For now, he's telling clients that keeping track of their employee demographics is a smart business move, whether the government requires it or not.
label: OBJ

text: Without it, he says, a company has no way of knowing if it has a problem — whether it's recruiting from too narrow a pool, or has a bad manager somewhere, or is screening out qualified candidates for no good reason.
label: OBJ

text: "It's like you're driving a car without a dashboard.
label: SUBJ

text: You have no idea what's going [on]. Am I speeding?
label: SUBJ

text: Am I speeding? Am I not speeding?
label: SUBJ

text: Is my check-engine light on?" he says. "You have nothing."
label: OBJ

text: "You have nothing." He's been reminding clients that while priorities have shifted at the EEOC, federal civil rights laws haven't changed.
label: OBJ

text: "Stay within the law, and you will be okay," he says.

text: The far-right lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella won the first round of Colombia’s presidential election on Sunday and will face senator Iván Cepeda, the candidate backed by leftwing president Gustavo Petro, in the runoff.

text: With 99.97% of ballots counted, the outsider and Donald Trump admirer Espriella secured 43.7% of the vote – just over 10.3m votes – compared with 40.9% (about 9.6m votes) for Cepeda, a philosopher and human rights activist who has served as a senator since 2014.
label: OBJ

text: The two will face each other in a runoff on 21 June.
label: OBJ

text: Amid fierce criticism from opposition groups, Mexico’s senate has passed a constitutional amendment to include “foreign interference” as grounds to annul election results in the country.
label: OBJ

text: The bill, which was presented by the country’s president, Claudia Sheinbaum, defines foreign interference as “illicit financing, propaganda, the systematic dissemination of misinformation, digital manipulation, and the intervention of foreign governments or agencies”.
label: OBJ

text: But critics say that the broadness of the bill’s language means virtually anything could be used to annul the results of an election: an article in a British newspaper, a statement from a US official, a report from an international NGO.
label: OBJ

text: “This is one of the most egregious, alarming and retrograde pieces of legislation in Mexico’s young democratic history,” said Arturo Sarukhan, a former Mexican ambassador to the US, on X.
label: OBJ

text: “This law doesn’t prevent foreign interference.
label: OBJ

text: It hands the government a veto over election outcomes it doesn’t like.”
label: SUBJ

text: The amendment has already been passed by the lower house of congress and now needs to be ratified by a majority of Mexico’s 32 states.
label: OBJ

text: Sheinbaum’s Morena controls 24 statehouses.
label: OBJ

text: The bill comes as Mexico has faced increased pressure from the US on security, with Donald Trump repeatedly threatening to invade the country and tackle cartels.
label: OBJ

text: Last month, the US justice department indicted 10 current and former officials from the state of Sinaloa, including the governor, for ties to a powerful drug-trafficking group.
label: OBJ

text: The indictment of Rubén Rocha Moya, the governor of Sinaloa and a close ally of former president Andrés Manuel López Obrador (known as Amlo), sent shock waves across Mexico’s political establishment.
label: SUBJ

text: Sheinbaum has called for more evidence from the US before considering extradition.
label: OBJ

text: The Mexican president also doubled down on the importance of sovereignty and non-intervention since the indictment was made public.
label: OBJ

text: “All Mexicans should agree that there should be no foreign interference in elections in Mexico,” Sheinbaum said at a news conference on Thursday.
label: OBJ

text: “We must all agree that in Mexico, we Mexicans decide who governs us.”
label: OBJ

text: The bill comes as Mexico faces midterm elections next year, which could see the governing Morena party lose its stranglehold on power: it currently controls the presidency and both the upper and lower chambers of congress.
label: SUBJ

text: The bill would allow Mexico’s electoral court to toss out election results if it determines there was interference from an overseas organization, a foreign government or citizen.
label: OBJ

text: But the court was stripped of its independence under Amlo and is now largely aligned with Morena.
label: SUBJ

text: “If [Morena] wanted, they could allege foreign intervention and the court would rule in their favor,” said Carlos Bravo Regidor, a political analyst.

label: SUBJ

label: OBJ
